edges.description: Loss of APC function permits canonical WNT pathway activation.
WNT activation increases proliferative drive in intestinal epithelium.
Sustained epithelial proliferation drives early adenoma formation.
Established adenomas acquire cooperative PI3K/mTOR pathway activation.
intermediate_mechanisms: Additional oncogenic and microenvironmental pathway cooperation

edges.description: Repeated adenoma initiation and persistence produce high-burden colorectal polyposis.
WNT and mTOR pathway activity tracks with specific microbial signatures.
Co-activated signaling supports progression toward genomically unstable advanced lesions.
edges.target: Genomic instability accumulation across adenoma grades

edges.description: Microbiome-linked signaling perturbation may contribute to heterogeneous evolutionary trajectories.
edges.target: Genomic instability accumulation across adenoma grades

edges.description: Accumulated genomic instability promotes emergence of carcinoma-driving alterations.
edges.source: Genomic instability accumulation across adenoma grades
edges.target: TP53 driver emergence in advanced adenomas

edges.description: TP53 driver acquisition promotes malignant transition of advanced adenomas.
edges.source: TP53 driver emergence in advanced adenomas

edges.description: Malignant transition culminates in invasive colorectal cancer.

evidence.snippet: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is an autosomal dominant disease caused by pathogenic germline adenomatous polyposis coli mutation, and characterized with multiple adenomas in the colon and the rectum.

nodes.description: APC-initiated tumorigenesis proceeds through activation of canonical WNT signaling as an early mechanistic axis in FAP carcinogenesis.

evidence.snippet: The process of carcinogenesis in FAP-CRC supports the classical cancerization model, where an initial APC mutation leads to the activation of the WNT signaling pathway and CIN.

nodes.description: Early adenomatous lesions arise in colorectal mucosa and provide the substrate for subsequent lesion expansion and malignant progression.

evidence.snippet: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is characterized by the development of many tens to thousands of adenomas in the rectum and colon during the second decade of life.

nodes.description: Advanced adenomas progress to invasive colorectal carcinoma in the absence of effective preventive intervention.

evidence.snippet: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is a genetic syndrome characterized by multiple polyps at various evolutionary stages, which, if left untreated, inevitably progress to colorectal cancer (CRC).

nodes.description: In unmanaged disease, colorectal cancer risk is extremely high and often approaches certainty by mid-adulthood.

evidence.reference_title: Endoscopic and chemopreventive management of familial adenomatous polyposis syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Colorectal cancer rates reach 100% by the age of 45, making early colectomy a mainstay of treatment.

nodes.description: Classic FAP is defined by extensive colorectal adenoma burden, typically with hundreds of premalignant lesions.

evidence.snippet: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is a genetic disease causing hundreds of premalignant polyps in affected persons and is an ideal model to study transitions of early precancer states to colorectal cancer (CRC).

nodes.description: Precancer transitions in classic FAP involve strong pro-growth molecular remodeling and expanded epithelial proliferative signaling.

evidence.snippet: Transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic and lipidomic analyses revealed a dynamic choreography of thousands of molecular and cellular events that occur during precancerous transitions toward cancer formation.

nodes.description: Adenoma evolution in classic FAP is marked by increasing chromosomal and DNA repair instability features from tubular adenoma to villous adenoma to carcinoma.
nodes.id: Genomic instability accumulation across adenoma grades

evidence.snippet: Furthermore, a progressive increase in the HRD score (a measure of “genomic scars”) was observed from tubular adenomas to villous adenomas and ultimately to carcinomas.

Adenomas exhibited lower mutational rates than FAP-CRC and displayed recurrent alterations in well-known chromosomal instability (CIN) genes (APC, RAS, SMAD4 and TP53) and DNA damage repair genes (SUZ12, KMT2C, BCLAF1, RUNX1, and ARID1B), suggesting the presence of genomic instability.

nodes.description: Germline pathogenic APC variation establishes a constitutional tumor suppressor deficit that predisposes colonic and rectal epithelium to adenoma development.

evidence.snippet: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is an autosomal dominant disease caused by pathogenic germline adenomatous polyposis coli mutation, and characterized with multiple adenomas in the colon and the rectum.

evidence.reference_title: Desmoid Tumors in Familial Adenomatous Polyposis.
evidence.snippet: Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) is a cancer syndrome caused by a germline mutation in the adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) gene.

nodes.description: Metformin remains investigational in FAP, with mixed efficacy signals across studies and evidence of pathway-level biologic activity.

evidence.snippet: One-year metformin therapy for FAP is safe and effective, potentially mediated by modulating the intestinal flora.

evidence.reference_title: The Effect of Metformin in Treatment of Adenomas in Patients with Familial Adenomatous Polyposis.
evidence.snippet: A 7-month metformin treatment (500 mg or 1,500 mg) did not reduce the number or size of polyps in the colorectum or duodenum of FAP patients as compared to placebo.

nodes.description: Distinct fecal and lesion-associated microbial signatures correlate with WNT and mTOR pathway readouts in FAP, indicating microbiome-linked modulation of progression biology.

evidence.snippet: Our study reveals an interplay between Wnt/β-catenin and PI3K/mTOR, whose derangement correlates with specific microbiota signatures in FAP and CRC patients, and identifies new potential biomarkers and targets to improve CRC prevention, early adenoma detection and treatment.

nodes.description: NSAID-based chemoprevention is used as an adjunctive approach but evidence remains heterogeneous and not sufficient to replace surgery/surveillance.

evidence.snippet: The use of chemopreventive agents as an adjunct to these measures has long been studied both in the general population and in hereditary cancer patients, in whom the risk of malignancy, and therefore the potential risk reduction, is considerably greater.
However, to date only few compounds have been found to be effective, safe, and tolerable for widespread use.

nodes.description: FAP lesions demonstrate PI3K/mTOR pathway co-activation alongside WNT dysregulation, consistent with pathway cooperation during progression.

evidence.snippet: We found simultaneous hyperactivation of Wnt/β-catenin and PI3K/mTOR pathways in FAP-lesions compared to CRCs.

nodes.description: Advanced FAP lesions accumulate TP53 driver events that prime transition to invasive carcinoma.
nodes.id: TP53 driver emergence in advanced adenomas

evidence.snippet: TP53 emerged as the primary driver gene for adenoma-carcinoma transition, with driver mutations consistently appearing simultaneously rather than sequentially acquired from adenomas to carcinomas.
